- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# /optimize skill configuration - template
#
# Copy this to config.toml and edit for your target. The skill NEVER reads
# literal paths, flags, VLENs, hostnames, or CPU names from SKILL.md or from
# scripts under script/. Everything flows through this file.
#
# Adding a new VPU configuration:
#   1. Drop the Andes Verilog config file under fpga_config/
#   2. If the microarchitecture family is new, add references/uarch/<soc>.md
#   3. Edit [target] to point at the new .inc and uarch doc
#   4. Adjust [target.cflags_base] and [qemu].cpu / [qemu].vlen
#
# No SKILL.md or script edits should ever be needed for a new target.

################################################################################
# [repo] - where libnn source lives and where FSM state is persisted
################################################################################
[repo]
# Absolute path to the libnn source tree (build.sh, Source/, Include/, etc.)
libnn_root = "/home/nick/work/libnn_0421"

# State file location, relative to libnn_root (skill writes FSM state here)
state_file = ".claude/skills/optimize/state.json"

# Work directory for intermediate artifacts (compiled ELFs, objdumps, logs),
# relative to libnn_root
work_dir = ".claude/skills/optimize/work"


################################################################################
# [toolchain] - cross-compiler
################################################################################
[toolchain]
bin_dir = "/local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast540/build-toolchain/linux/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
prefix  = "riscv64-elf-"


################################################################################
# [target] - which VPU, its hardware profile, and its build flags
################################################################################
[target]
# Target CPU name (passed to build.sh)
cpu = "AX45MPV"

# Andes Verilog config file (parsed for VLEN, caches, ISA feature flags).
# Relative to the skill directory (.claude/skills/optimize/).
fpga_config = "fpga_config/ax45mpv_v1024_8mb.inc"

# Microarchitecture reference doc (pipeline model, stall taxonomy).
# Proposer subagent reads this during `analyze`. Relative to skill dir.
uarch_ref = "references/uarch/andes_45_series.md"

# Intrinsic API reference doc (which macros are legal in new RVV code).
# Implement subagent reads this. Relative to skill dir.
intrinsic_api = "references/intrinsic_api/nds_vec_macros.md"


# CFLAGS assembled from three sources (order preserved, concatenated):
#   cflags_base    - baseline compile flags (both verify and perf builds)
#   cflags_perf    - extra flags for FPGA perf build (adds PF_COUNTER, etc.)
#   cflags_verify  - extra flags for QEMU correctness build

[target.cflags_base]
# Flags extracted from build.sh's AX45MPV block
flags = [
  "-O3", "-ffunction-sections", "-fdata-sections", "-Wall", "-Werror",
  "-mtune=andes-45-series", "-fno-strict-aliasing",
  "-mext-vector", "-DENA_VEC_ISA",
  "-DMAX_VLEN=1024", "-DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000",
  "-DENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD",
  "-fno-tree-slp-vectorize", "-fno-tree-vectorize",
]

[target.cflags_perf]
flags = [
  "-DPF_COUNTER", "-DENA_TEST_PERF", "-DENA_MEASURE_PERF=1",
  "-DENA_RUN_TWICE", "-DENA_CACHEABLE_ENV_SETUP",
  "-mvh",
]

[target.cflags_verify]
flags = [
  "-DENA_FAST_ALGO", "-DENA_DUMP_GOLDEN=0", "-DENA_MEASURE_PERF=0",
  "-mext-vector=zve64d",
]


[target.active_defines]
# Macros analyze/implement subagents treat as defined or undefined for this
# build (governs which #ifdef branches are live source vs dead code).
defined   = ["ENA_VEC_ISA", "ENA_TILING", "ENA_FAST_ALGO",
             "ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD"]
undefined = ["ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC", "ENA_VEC_ISA_ZVQMAC"]


################################################################################
# [qemu] - correctness simulator (ground truth: accuracy only, not perf)
################################################################################
[qemu]
binary  = "/local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv64"
machine = "andes_ae350"
cpu     = "andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024"
# QEMU device config, relative to libnn_root
config  = "project/ax45mpv/qemu_cfg/zve64d/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C_dw0_mw1.cfg"
vlen    = 1024
timeout = 300


################################################################################
# [fpga] - ground truth for cycle counts
################################################################################
[fpga]
host    = "sw-boards.andestech.com"
port    = 1119
timeout = 300

# Linker script + sim-wrapper + reset flag, all relative to libnn_root
linker_script   = "project/ax45mpv/ae350-xip_hvm.ld"
sim_wrapper     = "project/ax45mpv/riscv64-sim-wrapper-on-board"
reset_flag_file = "project/ax45mpv/First_Reset_done"


################################################################################
# [limits] - FSM exit conditions and cache TTLs
################################################################################
[limits]
# Max optimization rounds per function
max_rounds = 10
# Re-validation cache: skip validate state if config + .inc unchanged and
# last validation was more recent than this (hours). 0 = always re-validate.
validate_cache_hours = 24

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
# Retest baseline on QEMU with -mvh now in cflags_verify
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_qemu_test.sh relu_s8 --strace --timeout 120 2>&1 | tail -15

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: **Verify PASS** (3/3 sub-tests). Emit verify trace, transition to measure, run FPGA.

input.command:
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
CONFIG_TOML="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/config.toml"
source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh" "${CONFIG_TOML}"

cat > /tmp/verify_trace.json <<'EOF'
{"result": "PASS", "sub_tests": 3, "stderr": ""}
EOF
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step verify /tmp/verify_trace.json 2>&1 | tail -1

python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" "measure" "${SCRIPT_DIR}" <<'PYEOF'
import json, os, sys, subprocess
from datetime import datetime
f, new_step, script_dir = sys.argv[1:4]
state = json.load(open(f))
prev_step = state.get("step","idle")
now = datetime.now()
prev_start = datetime.fromisoformat(state.get("prev_step_started", now.isoformat()))
elapsed = (now - prev_start).total_seconds()
subprocess.run([os.path.join(script_dir,"emit_trace.sh"),"timing","--fn",state.get("function",""),
                "--round",str(state.get("round",0)),prev_step,f"{elapsed:.3f}"],check=False)
state.update({"step": new_step, "step_started": now.isoformat(), "prev_step_started": now.isoformat()})
state["history"] = state.get("history", []) + [f"{now.isoformat()} {new_step}"]
json.dump(state, open(f,"w"), indent=2)
PYEOF
echo "=== FPGA measure ==="
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_fpga_test.sh relu_s8 2>&1 | tail -15
input.description: Trace verify, transition measure, run FPGA

content.text: Regression: 2984→3058 cycles (0.976x), 333→390 insts. The uarch doesn't overlap same-register VLSU across iters. Emitting traces and recording the lesson.

input.command:
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
CONFIG_TOML="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/config.toml"
source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh" "${CONFIG_TOML}"

cat > /tmp/measure_trace.json <<'EOF'
{"cycles": 3058, "instrs": 390, "ipc": 0.127, "speedup": 0.976, "cycles_before": 2984}
EOF
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step measure /tmp/measure_trace.json 2>&1 | tail -1
echo "cycles regressed: 2984 -> 3058 (speedup 0.976x). Added prologue/epilogue + fallback branch; VLSU did not overlap store->load on same v0/v8/v16/v24 across iters." > /tmp/regression.txt
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh fail regression /tmp/regression.txt 2>&1 | tail -1

python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" "learn" "${SCRIPT_DIR}" <<'PYEOF'
import json, os, sys, subprocess
from datetime import datetime
f, new_step, script_dir = sys.argv[1:4]
state = json.load(open(f))
prev_step = state.get("step","idle")
now = datetime.now()
prev_start = datetime.fromisoformat(state.get("prev_step_started", now.isoformat()))
elapsed = (now - prev_start).total_seconds()
subprocess.run([os.path.join(script_dir,"emit_trace.sh"),"timing","--fn",state.get("function",""),
                "--round",str(state.get("round",0)),prev_step,f"{elapsed:.3f}"],check=False)
state.update({
    "step": new_step,
    "cycles_after": "3058",
    "instrs_after": "390",
    "speedup": "0.976",
    "step_started": now.isoformat(),
    "prev_step_started": now.isoformat(),
})
state["round_history"] = state.get("round_history", []) + [{
    "round": 1,
    "approach": "2-iter software pipeline (prologue load, body store-prev+load-next+vmax, epilogue store)",
    "cycles": 3058,
    "instrs": 390,
    "ipc": 0.127,
    "arith_intensity": 0.5,
    "reuse_rate": 1.0,
    "speedup": 0.976,
    "outcome": "regressed",
    "insight": "AX45MPV VLSU does not overlap store->load across iterations when the same register group (v0/v8/v16/v24 at m8) is reused. Added prologue/epilogue and fallback branch cost 57 extra insts without any cycle gain. For memory-bandwidth-bound kernels using all m8 regs, pure SW-pipelining is net negative."
}]
json.dump(state, open(f,"w"), indent=2)
PYEOF
echo "state -> learn"

content.text: Learn step: writing wiki lesson. Let me check the wiki-writer skill.

input.command:
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
ls ${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/ 2>/dev/null
echo "---"
find ${LIB_ROOT}/.claude -name "libnn-wiki-writer*" -o -name "wiki-writer*" 2>/dev/null | head
echo "---"
ls ~/.claude/skills/libnn-wiki-writer/ 2>/dev/null
cat ${LIB_ROOT}/docs/wiki/SCHEMA.md 2>/dev/null | head -40

content.content:
optimize
---
---
SKILL.md
---
type: meta
---

# Wiki Schema

## Purpose

This wiki is the knowledge store for the `/optimize` skill. It replaces the ChromaDB optdb. All optimization lessons, technique pages, and operator profiles live here as plain Markdown files with YAML frontmatter.

## Directory Layout

```

content.content:
---
name: libnn-wiki-writer
description: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
type: internal
---

# Wiki Writer

Internal skill: called by `/libnn-optimizer` during the `learn` state. Defines style rules, page templates, and update operations for `docs/wiki/`.

## Purpose

Wiki pages serve two audiences: humans reading them for context, and the `/libnn-optimizer` agent reading them as structured memory. Pages must be clear to both. The failure mode to avoid is jargon-only writing -- pages that assume the reader already knows what GEMM, LMUL, or vd4dots means.

**Three core principles:**

1. **Define every acronym on first use.** Write "GEMM (General Matrix Multiply)" the first time, then "GEMM" after.
2. **Lead with plain-English summary.** The first content section of every page must explain what this is in terms any engineer understands, with no assumed domain knowledge.
3. **Use concrete numbers, not vague words.** "2.1x speedup (4,823 to 2,298 cycles)" not "significant improvement". "IPC of 1.04 when the theoretical max is 2.0" not "low IPC".

---

## Jargon Glossary

Reference table for constructing inline definitions. Do not paste these verbatim -- incorporate them naturally into sentences.

| Term | Plain-English Expansion |
|------|------------------------|
| GEMM | General Matrix Multiply -- multiplying two matrices together; the core math inside convolution |
| im2col | "image to column" -- a transform that reshapes a 3D image patch into a flat row so convolution becomes a matrix multiply |
| LMUL | "length multiplier" -- how many physical vector registers are ganged together into one logical register; higher LMUL = more elements per instruction but fewer usable register names |
| VL | "vector length" -- how many elements the CPU actually processes in one instruction; set by vsetvli |
| VLEN | hardware vector register width in bits (e.g., 1024 bits = 128 bytes per register) |
| vsetvli | "vector set vector length immediate" -- instruction that sets VL and SEW for the next block of vector code |
| vd4dots | Andes custom dot-product instruction: computes 4 partial dot products per cycle using 4-element int8 groups |
| SEW | "selected element width" -- the bit width of one element in a vector register (8, 16, 32, or 64 bits) |
| VLSU | "vector load/store unit" -- the hardware pipeline stage that handles vector memory operations |
| IPC | "instructions per cycle" -- how many instructions finish per clock; theoretical max on AX45MPV is 2.0 for most code |
| VQ | vector quarter-width -- element width is VLEN/4 |
| VD | vector double-width -- element width is VLEN*2 |
| VC | vector compressed -- used in vector compress/expand operations |
| VW | "vector widening" -- an instruction that produces a result twice as wide as its inputs (e.g., 8-bit inputs, 16-bit output) |
| dual-issue | AX45MPV can retire two instructions per cycle when they have no data dependency and use different execution units |
| VREDSUM | "vector reduction sum" -- collapses all elements in a vector register into a single scalar by adding them |
| tiling | splitting a large computation into smaller blocks that fit in registers; trades loop overhead for better register reuse |

---

## Style Rules with Before/After Examples

### Rule 1: Explain technique impact with an analogy, not just a metric

**Before (jargon):**
> Switched from LMUL=1 to LMUL=4, increasing register utilization from 25% to 100%.

**After (naive expression):**
> The vector unit is like a bus with 128 seats. With LMUL=1 at VLEN=1024 processing int8, each instruction uses all 128 seats. But the old code used LMUL=4 with int32, which means only 32 elements per register -- like running that bus with 32 passengers. We switched to packing 4x more work per instruction.

The analogy makes the performance lever visible. Lead with the analogy, follow with the numbers.

### Rule 2: Name anti-pattern symptoms in plain terms

**Before (jargon):**
> vd4dots exhibits poor utilization when K < 16 due to partial group completion.

**After (naive expression):**
> vd4dots works by grouping 4 input channels at a time. When the number of input channels (K) is less than 16, most groups are incomplete -- like a conveyor belt that moves 4 items at a time but only has 1-3 items on most trips. The hardware still pays the full cost per group, so efficiency drops sharply below K=16.

### Rule 3: Explain what pipeline numbers mean, not just what they are

**Before (jargon):**
> Profile showed IPC=1.04 with high VLSU stalls, indicating memory-bound execution.

**After (naive expression):**
> The profiler measured 1.04 instructions completed per clock cycle. The AX45MPV can theoretically do 2.0 per cycle, so we are using about half the available throughput. The stall log showed the vector load/store unit (VLSU) was blocked waiting for data from memory on nearly every other cycle -- the computation was fast but data was arriving too slowly to keep it fed.

### Rule 4: Code comments must explain purpose, not restate the instruction

**Before:**
```c

---
type: operator
function: riscv_nn_avepool_HWC_s8
source: Source/PoolingFunctions/riscv_nn_avepool_HWC_s8.c
tags: [avepool, s8, rvv, e16, accumulation]
current_speedup: 2.78x
baseline_cycles: 149131
current_cycles: 53613
---

# avepool_HWC_s8

## Function Signature

```c
riscv_nn_status riscv_nn_avepool_HWC_s8(
    const int8_t *in_tensor,
    const uint16_t in_tensor_dim,
    const uint16_t in_tensor_ch,
    const uint16_t filt_dim,
    const uint16_t padding,
    const uint16_t stride,
    const uint16_t out_tensor_dim,
    int8_t *in_tmp_buf,
    int8_t *out_tensor
);
```

## What This Function Does

---LOG---

**riscv_nn_conv_1x1_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_fast_any** - Round 2 (runtime dispatch)
- Approach: VLMAX-threshold dispatch: VD4DOTS for small N, tiling for large N
- Result: Fixes regression on large-N test cases; keeps 2.60x for small N
- Lesson: VD4DOTS only wins when N < VLMAX_e8_m4/4. See [[runtime-dispatch]].

**riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any_dilated** - Round 1
- Approach: groups==1 fast path with unroll4 GEMM (4-pixel batching)
- Result: 1.05x

**riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any_dilated** - Round 2
- Approach: pad=0 fast path skipping boundary checks, precomputed dilation strides
- Result: 1.24x (cumulative 1.30x from baseline: 1,822,840 -> 1,398,877 cycles)
- Lesson: Boundary check elimination via pad=0 specialization. See [[im2col-strategies]].

---

## 2026-04-17

- **riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any** round 1 | vmulh requantization (single M8 pass, no vwmul split) | 174,549 -> 164,347 cycles (1.06x) | improved | Lesson: vmulh (vector multiply-high, returns upper 32 bits of 32x32 multiply) stays at e32 width, avoiding the e64 widening that forces splitting an M8 accumulator (LMUL=8, using all register groups) into two M4 halves. Saves ~14 vector ops per requantization. See [[vmulh-requantization]].

---
type: operator
function: riscv_nn_relu_s8
source: Source/ActivationFunctions/riscv_nn_relu_s8.c
tags: [relu, s8, rvv, activation, memory-bound]
current_speedup: 1.00x
baseline_cycles: 2984
current_cycles: 2984
---

# relu_s8

## Function Signature

```c
void riscv_nn_relu_s8(q7_t *in_out, uint32_t size);
```

## What This Function Does

Applies ReLU (rectified linear unit) in place over a flat buffer of `size` int8 elements. Each element is replaced with `max(x, 0)`. No bias, no scale, no requantization. The computation per element is a single compare-and-mask.

## Active Code Path

`#elif defined(ENA_VEC_ISA)` branch at lines 104-150 in the original source. The other branch (`ENA_VEC_ISA && ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC`) is dead code because `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` is undefined for this build target (see `config.toml` `[target.active_defines].undefined`). The active path uses `NDS_VEC_*` macros that expand to raw inline `asm volatile` blocks -- not `__riscv_*` C intrinsics.

Structure:
1. Select LMUL: m8 when DLEN:VLEN is 1:1, m4 when DLEN is half of VLEN. On AX45MPV at VLEN=1024/DLEN=1024, runs at m8.
2. Main loop: 4x unrolled, `vle8.v / vmax.vx 0 / vse8.v` on v0, v8, v16, v24.
3. Tail loop: single `vsetvl / vle8 / vmax / vse8` for the remainder.

At m8 with VLEN=1024, one "block" is `4 * 1024 = 4096` bytes.

## Test Shapes

`Examples/unit_func/t_relu_s8.c` runs three sizes: 3, 71, 65535. PF_COUNTER is enabled only for the 65535-byte case. That is the one this page's cycle counts reflect.

## Baseline Analysis (round 0)

- Cycles: 2984, Instructions: 333, IPC: 0.11
- Throughput: 131,070 bytes (read+write of 65535 elements) in 2984 cycles = **43.9 bytes per cycle**.
- The L2 data bus is 512 bits wide = 64 bytes per cycle of raw capacity, but the VLSU must time-share loads and stores on that port, so the effective per-direction ceiling is roughly 32 bytes per cycle. Measured 43.9 B/cyc implies load and store are interleaving well under the hood.
- The function is memory-bandwidth-bound. Each instruction already processes 1024 bytes of data (one m8 operation at VLEN=1024), which is why IPC is so low in absolute terms -- more bytes per instruction means fewer instructions, not an inefficient pipeline. See [[ipc-vs-throughput]].
- Buffer is 65535 bytes (64 KB), which is larger than L1 D-Cache (32 KB). After the first half fills L1, the kernel streams from L2 for the rest.

## Round History

### Round 1 -- regressed: 0.976x

**Approach:** Two-iteration software pipeline (prologue, pipelined body, epilogue). The intent was to let stores of block N-1 drain on the vector load-store unit (VLSU) while loads for block N issue, overlapping the two directions.

**Structure:**
- Prologue: load block 0 into v0/v8/v16/v24 and vmax.
- Body (while 2 blocks remain): store current block from v0..v24, load next block into the same v0..v24, vmax.
- Epilogue: store the final block.
- Fallback original 4x-unroll for the case of exactly one remaining block.

**Result:** 2984 -> 3058 cycles (+74). 333 -> 390 instructions (+57). The extra 57 instructions came from the prologue, epilogue, and the fallback branch. No cycle gain materialized.

**Why:** The pipelined body's stores and the next-iteration's loads target the same register group (v0/v8/v16/v24 -- at m8 this is the only valid grouping). The AX45MPV VLSU serializes these through the store-queue and register file: loads cannot write to v0 while a prior store is still draining v0. Cross-iteration overlap did not happen.

**Lesson:** Pure software pipelining does not help a memory-bound kernel whose only available register group is already reused across iterations. Either a different register group must be available (not possible at m8/VLEN=1024) or a different technique (cache prefetch, widening unroll, algorithmic change) must cut the traffic. See [[anti-sw-pipeline-same-reg-group]].

## Open Directions

- **Cache prefetch.** Issue `prefetch.r` for `in_out + 2 * maxvl_per_loop` at the top of each main iteration. Would hide L2 miss latency without adding register pressure. Worth trying next.
- **Accept 43.9 B/cyc as near-ceiling.** Per [[memory-bound-diagnosis]], if prefetch also returns neutral or small, the kernel is at the bandwidth wall and further work has diminishing returns.

## Active Code Path

`#elif defined(ENA_VEC_ISA)` branch at lines 104-150 in the original source. The other branch (`ENA_VEC_ISA && ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC`) is dead code because `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` is undefined for this build target (see `config.toml` `[target.active_defines].undefined`). The active path uses `NDS_VEC_*` macros that expand to raw inline `asm volatile` blocks -- not `__riscv_*` C intrinsics.

Structure:
1. Select LMUL: m8 when DLEN:VLEN is 1:1, m4 when DLEN is half of VLEN. On AX45MPV at VLEN=1024/DLEN=1024, runs at m8.
2. Main loop: 4x unrolled, `vle8.v / vmax.vx 0 / vse8.v` on v0, v8, v16, v24.
3. Tail loop: single `vsetvl / vle8 / vmax / vse8` for the remainder.

At m8 with VLEN=1024, one "block" is `4 * 1024 = 4096` bytes.

## Test Shapes

`Examples/unit_func/t_relu_s8.c` runs three sizes: 3, 71, 65535. PF_COUNTER is enabled only for the 65535-byte case. That is the one this page's cycle counts reflect.

## Baseline Analysis (round 0)

- Cycles: 2984, Instructions: 333, IPC: 0.11
- Throughput: 131,070 bytes (read+write of 65535 elements) in 2984 cycles = **43.9 bytes per cycle**.
- The L2 data bus is 512 bits wide = 64 bytes per cycle of raw capacity, but the VLSU must time-share loads and stores on that port, so the effective per-direction ceiling is roughly 32 bytes per cycle. Measured 43.9 B/cyc implies load and store are interleaving well under the hood.
- The function is memory-bandwidth-bound. Each instruction already processes 1024 bytes of data (one m8 operation at VLEN=1024), which is why IPC is so low in absolute terms -- more bytes per instruction means fewer instructions, not an inefficient pipeline. See [[ipc-vs-throughput]].
- Buffer is 65535 bytes (64 KB), which is larger than L1 D-Cache (32 KB). After the first half fills L1, the kernel streams from L2 for the rest.

## Round History

### Round 1 -- regressed: 0.976x

**Approach:** Two-iteration software pipeline (prologue, pipelined body, epilogue). The intent was to let stores of block N-1 drain on the vector load-store unit (VLSU) while loads for block N issue, overlapping the two directions.

**Structure:**
- Prologue: load block 0 into v0/v8/v16/v24 and vmax.
- Body (while 2 blocks remain): store current block from v0..v24, load next block into the same v0..v24, vmax.
- Epilogue: store the final block.
- Fallback original 4x-unroll for the case of exactly one remaining block.

**Result:** 2984 -> 3058 cycles (+74). 333 -> 390 instructions (+57). The extra 57 instructions came from the prologue, epilogue, and the fallback branch. No cycle gain materialized.

**Why:** The pipelined body's stores and the next-iteration's loads target the same register group (v0/v8/v16/v24 -- at m8 this is the only valid grouping). The AX45MPV VLSU serializes these through the store-queue and register file: loads cannot write to v0 while a prior store is still draining v0. Cross-iteration overlap did not happen.

**Lesson:** Pure software pipelining does not help a memory-bound kernel whose only available register group is already reused across iterations. Either a different register group must be available (not possible at m8/VLEN=1024) or a different technique (cache prefetch, widening unroll, algorithmic change) must cut the traffic. See [[anti-sw-pipeline-same-reg-group]].

## Open Directions

- **Cache prefetch.** Issue `prefetch.r` for `in_out + 2 * maxvl_per_loop` at the top of each main iteration. Would hide L2 miss latency without adding register pressure. Worth trying next.
- **Accept 43.9 B/cyc as near-ceiling.** Per [[memory-bound-diagnosis]], if prefetch also returns neutral or small, the kernel is at the bandwidth wall and further work has diminishing returns.

# Anti-pattern: software pipelining with a reused register group

## What This Is About

Software pipelining is a loop-transformation where you issue the loads for iteration N+1 before issuing the stores of iteration N, so the vector load/store unit (VLSU) can work on both directions of memory traffic at once. On paper it hides store latency behind the next iteration's load latency, buying a few cycles per iteration.

It only works if the two phases use **different vector registers**, so the next iteration's load has a free register to write into while the store is still draining the old register.

## When It Backfires

On the Andes AX45MPV at VLEN=1024, LMUL=m8 is the only way to get VL=1024 at int8 SEW. An m8 operation consumes 8 consecutive vector registers and the legal groupings are only v0, v8, v16, v24 -- four groups total. If the loop is already 4x unrolled using all four groups (as in relu_s8 after using v0/v8/v16/v24), there is **no spare register group** for the pipelined next iteration to land its loads into. You end up reusing v0/v8/v16/v24 across iterations.

When the next-iteration's load targets the same register group that the previous iteration's store just left, the VLSU has to fully drain the store-queue entry for that register before it will accept a new load into it. There is no cross-iteration overlap. All you added is prologue, epilogue, and a fallback-branch for small remainders -- pure code bloat.

## Symptoms

- Cycles go up slightly (1-3%).
- Instruction count goes up by a fixed amount that matches the prologue+epilogue+fallback size (in relu_s8 round 1: +57 instructions).
- Measured bytes-per-cycle does not change. Throughput is still pinned at the L2 bandwidth wall.
- The VLSU was already at the memory-bandwidth ceiling -- the real bottleneck was L2 throughput, not store-to-load latency.

## How to Recognize the Situation Before You Code

Ask three questions:

1. At this LMUL and VLEN, how many register groups exist? At m8 VLEN=1024 there are four (v0/v8/v16/v24).
2. How many does the current unrolled loop already use? If the answer is "all of them", pipelining has no free register landing pad.
3. Is the kernel already memory-bandwidth-bound (bytes/cycle near L2_DATA_WIDTH)? If yes, the problem is not store-load latency.

If the register groups are fully used AND the kernel is bandwidth-bound, skip software pipelining. Try cache prefetch, algorithmic traffic reduction, or accept the wall.

## What To Do Instead

- **Cache prefetch:** Issue prefetch for one or two cache lines ahead of the current pointer. Hides L2 miss latency without needing extra vector registers.
- **Reduce traffic:** Can the algorithm process data in a way that touches fewer bytes? For pure activation (relu), the answer is no -- you must read every byte and write every byte.
- **Accept the ceiling:** Per [[memory-bound-diagnosis]], when bytes/cycle is within ~15% of L2_DATA_WIDTH, declare the function done.

## Contrast: When Software Pipelining Does Help

- Lower LMUL (m1, m2, m4) leaves spare register groups. Pipelining works if those spares are used for the next iteration.
- Compute-bound kernels where the store->load hazard is measurable in cycles (rare for elementwise int8).
- Functions that mix different data types across registers (e.g., int8 input and int16 accumulator) so the pipeline stages land on different register banks.

## Seen In

- relu_s8 round 1: 2984 -> 3058 cycles (0.976x). Reverted.

## Related

- [[memory-bound-diagnosis]] -- how to tell you are at the L2 wall.
- [[ipc-vs-throughput]] -- why bytes/cycle beats IPC for vector kernels.
- [[lmul-selection]] -- why m8 at VLEN=1024 consumes all register groups.

# libnn Optimization Wiki

Reference for the `/optimize` skill. Read this file first in `select` to identify relevant pages, then read the linked pages.

---

## General Techniques

### GEMM Strategy

- [[tiling-gemm-to-vd4dots]] -- Replace ENA_TILING GEMM (vectorize N) with im2col + vd4dots GEMM (vectorize K). Applies to all conv_HWC_s8 sym variants. 4-5x speedup. **Start here for any conv_HWC function.**
- [[vectorize-k-vs-n]] -- When to vectorize K vs N: K wins when K >> VL. N wins when N >= VLMAX_e8_m4/4. Includes work-per-instruction analysis.
- [[im2col-strategies]] -- Efficient im2col: 5-region bulk copy (center region), pad=0 fast path for dilated, 4-pixel batching. Applied after GEMM kernel is correct.
- [[runtime-dispatch]] -- VLMAX-based threshold to select kernel at runtime. Avoids regressions when N is large.

### Requantization

- [[vmulh-requantization]] -- Use vmulh (multiply-high, stays at e32) instead of vwmul (widens to e64) for requantization when shift >= 32. Avoids M8-to-M4 split. 1.06x on depthwise conv.

### Accumulation and SEW

- [[e16-accumulation]] -- Use e16/M4 instead of e32/M2 when values fit in int16. Doubles VL for pooling accumulators. Applied to avepool.
- [[lmul-selection]] -- LMUL/SEW reference table. Principle: match VL to the vectorized dimension. M1 for vd4dots register blocking.

### Pipeline and Throughput

- [[ax45mpv-pipeline]] -- AX45MPV VPU pipeline stages (VQ/VD/VC/VW), dual-issue, stall patterns. Use when doing uarch-level optimization.
- [[ipc-vs-throughput]] -- IPC is misleading for vector kernels. Lower IPC + more work/instruction = faster. Always compare cycle counts.

### Memory-Bound Analysis

- [[memory-bound-diagnosis]] -- Symptoms (IPC ~0.30, neutral LMUL changes), diagnostic procedure, when to stop optimizing.

### Anti-Patterns

- [[anti-vd4dots-small-k]] -- Do NOT apply vd4dots when K < 32 (e.g., RGB conv with K=27). Direct conv wins.
- [[anti-lmul-reduction-memory-bound]] -- Increasing LMUL/parallelism does not help memory-bound functions. IPC improves but cycles stay flat.

---

## Operator Profiles

| Operator | Speedup | Key Technique | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| [[conv-hwc-s8-sym-bias-fast]] | 4.35x | [[tiling-gemm-to-vd4dots]] | Optimized 2026-03-23 |
| [[conv-hwc-s8-asym-bias-any]] | 4.97x | [[tiling-gemm-to-vd4dots]] + [[im2col-strategies]] | Optimized 2026-03-24 |
| [[conv-1x1-asym-bias-fast-any]] | 2.60x | [[vectorize-k-vs-n]] + [[runtime-dispatch]] | Optimized 2026-03-25 |
| [[conv-hwc-rgb-sym-bias-fast]] | 2.57x | Direct conv ([[anti-vd4dots-small-k]]) | Optimized 2026-03-23 |
| [[conv-hwc-rgb-sft-bias-fast]] | 2.31x (cifar10) | VQMACC direct conv ([[anti-vd4dots-small-k]]) | Optimized 2026-04-13 |
| [[conv-hwc-s8-sft-bias-fast]] | 1.09x conv2, 1.05x conv3 (cifar10) | 4-pixel batch [[tiling-gemm-to-vd4dots]] | Optimized 2026-04-13 |
| [[avepool-hwc-s8]] | 2.78x | [[e16-accumulation]] | Optimized 2026-03-23 |
| [[conv-hwc-asym-bias-any-dilated]] | 1.30x | [[im2col-strategies]] pad=0 | Optimized 2026-03-24 |
| [[maxpool-hwc-s8]] | ~1.0x | Memory-bound -- no further opportunity | Exhausted 2026-03-23 |
| [[fc-mat-vec-s16-s16-s8-sft-bias-fast]] | ~1.0x (gru) | Weight layout locks algorithm; N-vectorize blocked | Algorithm exhausted 2026-04-14 |
| [[fc-s8-s8-s8-sft-bias-fast]] | 1.107x (kws-dnn), 1.16x IP2/IP3 | 2-row-group outer batching (shared input load) | Round 1 improved 2026-04-14 |
| [[conv-dw-hwc-u8-u8-u8-asym-bias-any]] | 1.06x | [[vmulh-requantization]] | Optimized 2026-04-17 |

---

## Quick Lookup: Which Page to Read for a New Function

| Function pattern | Read first |
|---|---|
| `conv_HWC_*_sym_*` | [[tiling-gemm-to-vd4dots]], then operator page |
| `conv_HWC_*_asym_*` | [[tiling-gemm-to-vd4dots]], [[im2col-strategies]], operator page |
| `conv_HWC_*_sft_*` | [[conv-hwc-s8-sft-bias-fast]] (4-pixel batch with unroll4) |
| `conv_1x1_*` | [[vectorize-k-vs-n]], [[runtime-dispatch]], [[conv-1x1-asym-bias-fast-any]] |
| `conv_HWC_*_RGB_*` | [[anti-vd4dots-small-k]], [[conv-hwc-rgb-sym-bias-fast]], [[conv-hwc-rgb-sft-bias-fast]] |
| `avepool_*` | [[e16-accumulation]], [[avepool-hwc-s8]] |
| `maxpool_*` | [[memory-bound-diagnosis]], [[maxpool-hwc-s8]] |
| `conv_dw_HWC_*` | [[vmulh-requantization]], [[conv-dw-hwc-u8-u8-u8-asym-bias-any]] |
| Any conv with dilation | [[im2col-strategies]] (pad=0 path), [[conv-hwc-asym-bias-any-dilated]] |

+- [[anti-sw-pipeline-same-reg-group]] -- Software pipelining fails when m8 has already used all register groups; the next-iter load reuses the same group the prev-iter store is draining. Pure code bloat.

- [[im2col-strategies]] -- Efficient im2col: 5-region bulk copy (center region), pad=0 fast path for dilated, 4-pixel batching. Applied after GEMM kernel is correct.
- [[runtime-dispatch]] -- VLMAX-based threshold to select kernel at runtime. Avoids regressions when N is large.

### Requantization

- [[vmulh-requantization]] -- Use vmulh (multiply-high, stays at e32) instead of vwmul (widens to e64) for requantization when shift >= 32. Avoids M8-to-M4 split. 1.06x on depthwise conv.

### Accumulation and SEW

- [[e16-accumulation]] -- Use e16/M4 instead of e32/M2 when values fit in int16. Doubles VL for pooling accumulators. Applied to avepool.
- [[lmul-selection]] -- LMUL/SEW reference table. Principle: match VL to the vectorized dimension. M1 for vd4dots register blocking.

### Pipeline and Throughput

- [[ax45mpv-pipeline]] -- AX45MPV VPU pipeline stages (VQ/VD/VC/VW), dual-issue, stall patterns. Use when doing uarch-level optimization.
- [[ipc-vs-throughput]] -- IPC is misleading for vector kernels. Lower IPC + more work/instruction = faster. Always compare cycle counts.

### Memory-Bound Analysis

- [[memory-bound-diagnosis]] -- Symptoms (IPC ~0.30, neutral LMUL changes), diagnostic procedure, when to stop optimizing.

### Anti-Patterns

- [[anti-vd4dots-small-k]] -- Do NOT apply vd4dots when K < 32 (e.g., RGB conv with K=27). Direct conv wins.
- [[anti-lmul-reduction-memory-bound]] -- Increasing LMUL/parallelism does not help memory-bound functions. IPC improves but cycles stay flat.
- [[anti-sw-pipeline-same-reg-group]] -- Software pipelining fails when m8 has already used all register groups; the next-iter load reuses the same group the prev-iter store is draining. Pure code bloat.

---

## Operator Profiles

| [[conv-hwc-rgb-sft-bias-fast]] | 2.31x (cifar10) | VQMACC direct conv ([[anti-vd4dots-small-k]]) | Optimized 2026-04-13 |
| [[conv-hwc-s8-sft-bias-fast]] | 1.09x conv2, 1.05x conv3 (cifar10) | 4-pixel batch [[tiling-gemm-to-vd4dots]] | Optimized 2026-04-13 |
| [[avepool-hwc-s8]] | 2.78x | [[e16-accumulation]] | Optimized 2026-03-23 |

| [[conv-dw-hwc-u8-u8-u8-asym-bias-any]] | 1.06x | [[vmulh-requantization]] | Optimized 2026-04-17 |

python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" "analyze" "${SCRIPT_DIR}" <<'PYEOF'
import json, os, sys, subprocess
from datetime import datetime
f, new_step, script_dir = sys.argv[1:4]
state = json.load(open(f))
prev_step = state.get("step","idle")
now = datetime.now()
prev_start = datetime.fromisoformat(state.get("prev_step_started", now.isoformat()))
elapsed = (now - prev_start).total_seconds()
# bump round BEFORE timing emission so learn gets round-1 credit (block handles -1)
state["round"] = state.get("round",1) + 1
subprocess.run([os.path.join(script_dir,"emit_trace.sh"),"timing","--fn",state.get("function",""),
                "--round",str(state["round"]-1 if prev_step=="learn" else state["round"]),
                prev_step,f"{elapsed:.3f}"],check=False)
state.update({"step": new_step, "step_started": now.isoformat(), "prev_step_started": now.isoformat()})
state["history"] = state.get("history", []) + [f"{now.isoformat()} {new_step}"]
# init next round trace dir
subprocess.run([os.path.join(script_dir,"emit_trace.sh"),"init"], check=False)
json.dump(state, open(f,"w"), indent=2)
print(f"round = {state['round']}")
PYEOF

**Round 1 complete (regressed).** Summary:

| round | approach | cycles | instrs | IPC | speedup | outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (baseline) | 4x-unroll m8 on v0/v8/v16/v24 | 2984 | 333 | 0.11 | 1.00x | - |
| 1 | 2-iter SW-pipeline (overlap store-prev/load-next) | 3058 | 390 | 0.13 | **0.976x** | regressed, reverted |

Key insight recorded to wiki as `anti-sw-pipeline-same-reg-group`: at m8/VLEN=1024, all four register groups are already in use; the next-iter load reuses the group the prev-iter store is still draining, so VLSU never overlaps. Baseline is already at 43.9 B/cyc against ~32 B/cyc per-direction L2 ceiling — memory-bandwidth-bound.

Proceeding to round 2 analyze.

input.prompt:
You are the `analyze` proposer for libnn RVV optimization skill. Round 2 on `riscv_nn_relu_s8`.

## Constraints (strict)

- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash (ONLY for `grep` and `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/.claude/skills/optimize/script/rebuild_single.sh -E`).
- DO NOT edit files. DO NOT compile/test/QEMU/FPGA. `rebuild_single.sh -E` (preprocess-only) IS allowed.

## Current state

- Function: `riscv_nn_relu_s8`
- Source: `Source/ActivationFunctions/riscv_nn_relu_s8.c` (baseline restored after round 1 regression)
- Baseline FPGA: **cycles=2984, instrs=333, IPC=0.11** (size=65535)
- Throughput: 43.9 bytes/cycle (131070 bytes in 2984 cycles)
- L2_DATA_WIDTH=512 bits = 64 B/cyc raw; effective ~32 B/cyc per-direction when loads and stores share the port
- Target: AX45MPV VLEN=1024 DLEN=1024 L1D 32KB L2 8MB, at m8/VLEN=1024 all 4 register groups (v0/v8/v16/v24) are in use by the 4x-unrolled main loop

## Round 1 result (regressed, reverted)

SW-pipeline across iterations failed: stores of iter N-1 and loads of iter N target the SAME register group → VLSU serializes. Added 57 instructions (prologue + epilogue + fallback) and gained 0 cycles. Recorded in `docs/wiki/general/anti-sw-pipeline-same-reg-group.md`.

## Your job

Round 2 hypothesis. Read these first:

1. `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/docs/wiki/operators/relu-s8.md` (this function's profile)
2. `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/docs/wiki/general/anti-sw-pipeline-same-reg-group.md` (just-learned anti-pattern)
3. `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/docs/wiki/general/memory-bound-diagnosis.md`
4. `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/docs/wiki/general/anti-lmul-reduction-memory-bound.md`
5. `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/.claude/skills/optimize/references/uarch/andes_45_series.md` (skim for prefetch hint opcodes, L2 latency)
6. `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/.claude/skills/optimize/references/intrinsic_api/nds_vec_macros.md` (check if `NDS_VEC_PREFETCH` or similar macro exists)
7. `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/Source/ActivationFunctions/riscv_nn_relu_s8.c` (raw source, baseline; find the live `#elif defined(ENA_VEC_ISA)` branch)
8. `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/.claude/skills/optimize/work/riscv_nn_relu_s8.i` (preprocessed)
9. Assembly: `grep -A 200 '<riscv_nn_relu_s8>:' /home/nick/work/libnn_0421/libnn.objdump | head -250`

Also: grep `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/Source/` for any existing use of prefetch macros/asm to see idiomatic usage.

## Candidate ideas

Evaluate these with the wiki insights:

- **A. Hardware cache prefetch** (`prefetch.r` via `.insn` or `NDS_VEC_*` prefetch macro if it exists): issue prefetch for `in_out + K*maxvl_per_loop` (K=1 or 2) at top of main loop. Goal: hide L2 latency. This is the most promising next step per round 1's notes.
- **B. Widen unroll from 4x to 8x at m4** (uses spare register groups v0/v4/v8/v12/v16/v20/v24/v28). BUT per [[anti-lmul-reduction-memory-bound]] this type of change is neutral on bandwidth-bound kernels. Likely wasted round.
- **C. Combine prefetch + unroll (8x m4)** – only makes sense if (A) alone doesn't fully hide latency.
- **D. Exit to idle**: accept 43.9 B/cyc as at the wall. Per [[memory-bound-diagnosis]], when throughput is within 15% of L2 ceiling, further work has diminishing returns. 43.9 vs 64 theoretical is 69%. vs 32 per-direction: 137% (we are ABOVE one-direction L2 peak, meaning loads+stores ARE already overlapping at some level).
- **E. Other algorithmic change** (propose if you see one).

## Output format

Under 600 words. Sections:
- `## Re-verified active path` (line range)
- `## Assembly snapshot (current baseline)`
- `## Metrics` (AI, reuse_rate, B/cyc, IPC — restate from round 0 since code reverted)
- `## Ranked ideas` (with risk, which lines to edit)
- `## Decision gate`: if your top idea lacks strong rationale, recommend EXIT → idle. Otherwise pick it as the hypothesis.
- `## Hypothesis` (one paragraph)

Be honest about diminishing returns. If prefetch has no clear evidence of large L2 miss latency in this kernel (e.g., achieved 43.9 B/cyc already exceeds per-direction ceiling → load+store ARE overlapping at pipelined level → prefetch may not help much), say so.
